'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Categories]] to [[Category:11]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:11]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:C]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Bump-01-18]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawachi-no-Mikoto}}

'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 6]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Tier 4]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{draft categories| [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:tier 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Asuka-period male royals]] [[Category:Nara-period male royals]] [[Category:Chikuzen Province people]] [[Category:Bungo Province people]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawachi-no-Mikoto}}

See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, presumed to be the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子|12=draft}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)|12=draft}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|12=draft}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}} poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B|12=draft}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja}}) was an imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}} suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|12=draft}}, a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft}}. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王|12=draft}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|12=draft}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋|12=draft}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使|12=draft}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|12=draft}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|12=draft}}. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆|12=draft}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王|12=draft}}, found in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}, Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja}}), active during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, was the eldest son of {{ill|Prince Nagaya|ja|長皇子|12=draft}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=draft}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=draft}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|12=draft}} and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録|12=draft}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安|12=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王|12=draft}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄|12=draft}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=draft}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{draft categories| [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Asuka-period male royals]] [[Category:Nara-period male royals]] [[Category:Chikuzen Province people]] [[Category:Bungo Province people]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawachi-no-Mikoto}}

The name appears across different periods, from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}}, serving as the {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|12=draft}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|12=draft}}. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)|12=draft}}'''. * A princess of the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, the daughter of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|高市皇子|12=draft}} and a {{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}} poet. Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasumaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|12=draft}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋|12=draft}} to receive {{ill|envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使|12=draft}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Governor-General of Tsukushi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|12=draft}} under {{ill|Empress Jitō|ja|持統天皇|12=draft}}. In {{ill|714}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=draft}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=draft}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft}} and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * {{ill|Taketoshi Tsurugi|ja|竹内理三|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄|12=draft}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=draft}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Asuka-period male royals]] [[Category:Nara-period male royals]] [[Category:Chikuzen Province people]] [[Category:Bungo Province people]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawachi-no-Mikoto}}
