revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial family|qq=}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], serving as the {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|qq=}}'''. * A princess of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and a [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Joō|qq=}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/694|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|qq=}}, a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kudara|qq=}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中臣大島}} to receive {{ill|Silla envoy|lt=Silla envoy|lt=Envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/envoys from Silla|qq=}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the Rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Joō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, found in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/728|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), active during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], was the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. In {{ill|714|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/714|qq=}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unranked|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|qq=}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda’s descendants|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大原桜井}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kadobe|qq=}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * [[:en:Rizō Takeuchi|Taketoshi Tsurugi]], {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Hideo|qq=}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirano Kunio|qq=}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|qq=}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] ==Merged second translation== '''Prince Kawachi''' (河内王, ''Kawachi no Ōkimi'' or ''Kawachi no Ō'') refers to multiple Japanese [[imperial family|imperial princes]] (諸王, ''shokō'') who lived from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]]. He is also referred to as '''Prince Kawauchi''' (川内王). * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], who served as the [[Dazai no Sochi|Governor of Tsukushi]] in [[Kyushu]] during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)|Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An early [[Nara period]] imperial prince, presumed to be the first son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]], the son of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Nara period)]]'''. * A late Nara period imperial prince of unknown lineage. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)]]'''. * A Nara period princess, the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takahata]] and a poet of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. See '''[[Kawachi no Joō]]'''. * Other figures bearing the title of ''Prince Kawachi'' are known, but none are of particular note. == Prince Kawachi (Asuka period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - before [[:en:April 5|April 5]] in the 8th year of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:694|694]])) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although no direct genealogical records exist, analyses from works such as the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' and ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' suggest he was a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] through {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|qq=}}. He may have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Baekje|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Baekje|qq=}}, with potential descendants such as {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]]. However, he was not a member of the [[:en:Baekje|Baekje royal family]] of the Korean Peninsula but rather a descendant of Emperor Bidatsu.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea}} [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kogane Kofun (Kawaran-machi)
Nearby stands the monument of a poem by Te-mochi Princess.]] In the first year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ''[[朱鳥 (era)|Shuchō era]]'' ([[:en:686|686]]), Prince Kawachi was dispatched to [[Tsukushi Province]] with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakatomi no Ōshima|ja|藤原大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to host Kim Chisang, an envoy from [[Silla]]. At the time, he held the Rank of ''Jōkōshi''. In [[September]] of the same year, upon the emperor’s passing, Prince Kawachi delivered eulogies for the [[Ōtoneri Bureau|great chamberlain]] and was likely in charge of related offices. In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:689|689]]), he was appointed [[Dazai no Sochi]] in Tsukushi and was granted weapons and goods. In [[:en:690|690]], he oversaw the hosting of [[Silla]] envoys. In [[:en:692|692]], he dispatched [[Buddhist monks]] to spread [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] to [[:en:Ōsumi Province|Ōsumi Province]] and [[Ata Province]], as well as ordered the presentation of an [[Amitābha]] statue. Prince Kawachi passed away in [[March]] [[:en:694|694]] while serving in Tsukushi. He was posthumously awarded the Rank of ''Jōdai-shi'' on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], along with funeral offerings. His funeral elegies by {{ill|Te-mochi Princess|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Princess|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, presumed to be his wife, appear in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' Volume 3, poems 417-419. His supposed tomb, the '''Kogane Kofun''' in [[Kawaran-machi]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], was designated as his burial site by the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Ministry of the Imperial Household]] in [[:en:1894|1894]]. However, archaeological studies identify it as a late 6th-century round tumulus. == Prince Kawachi (Nara period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]), a Nara period prince, was the grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].Sawada Hiroshi, "Genealogy of Tenmu's Imperial Relatives" (''Kokushi Gaku'', No. 142, [[:en:1990|1990]]) In the 7th year of the [[Wadō]] era ([[:en:714|714]]), he was promoted from ''unranked'' to ''Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade'' by hereditary privilege. However, he left no notable records and passed away on [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]. While the ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' lists him as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]], these claims conflict with primary sources. == Confusion in Genealogical Records == Although these two figures, Prince Kawachi of the Asuka and Nara periods, lived in different eras, their genealogies were historically conflated. The ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' mistakenly lists {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers as descendants of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].For example, see Yoshikawa Kōbunkan’s ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary'', or annotations in the ''Shin Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei'' edition of the ''Shoku Nihongi''. Modern scholarship, particularly by {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Takashi|qq=}}, corrects this confusion by separating the two figures. He identifies the Asuka Prince Kawachi as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers, while listing the Nara Prince Kawachi as the son of Prince Nagaya.Tanaka Takashi, "On the Genealogy of Prince Takayasu," in ''Tanaka Takashi Collected Works,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * [[Takeuchi Risan]], [[Yamada Hideo]], [[Hirano Kunio]], eds. ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary,'' Volume 2. [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], [[:en:1959|1959]]. * [[Tanaka Takashi]], "Debates on Imperial Genealogies," in ''Collected Works of Tanaka Takashi,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kawachi no Ōkimi]] [[ja:河内王]] [[sn:awachi no Ōkimi]] [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Nara period]] [[Category:People of Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:People of Buzen Province]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Asuka period]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial family|qq=}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], serving as the {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|qq=}}'''. * A princess of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and a [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Joō|qq=}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/694|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|qq=}}, a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kudara|qq=}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中臣大島}} to receive {{ill|Silla envoy|lt=Silla envoy|lt=Envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/envoys from Silla|qq=}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the Rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Joō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, found in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/728|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), active during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], was the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. In {{ill|714|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/714|qq=}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unranked|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|qq=}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda’s descendants|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大原桜井}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kadobe|qq=}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * [[:en:Rizō Takeuchi|Taketoshi Tsurugi]], {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Hideo|qq=}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirano Kunio|qq=}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|qq=}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] ==Merged second translation== '''Prince Kawachi''' (河内王, ''Kawachi no Ōkimi'' or ''Kawachi no Ō'') refers to multiple Japanese [[imperial family|imperial princes]] (諸王, ''shokō'') who lived from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]]. He is also referred to as '''Prince Kawauchi''' (川内王). * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], who served as the [[Dazai no Sochi|Governor of Tsukushi]] in [[Kyushu]] during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)|Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An early [[Nara period]] imperial prince, presumed to be the first son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]], the son of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Nara period)]]'''. * A late Nara period imperial prince of unknown lineage. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)]]'''. * A Nara period princess, the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takahata]] and a poet of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. See '''[[Kawachi no Joō]]'''. * Other figures bearing the title of ''Prince Kawachi'' are known, but none are of particular note. == Prince Kawachi (Asuka period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - before [[:en:April 5|April 5]] in the 8th year of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:694|694]])) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although no direct genealogical records exist, analyses from works such as the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' and ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' suggest he was a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] through {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|qq=}}. He may have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Baekje|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Baekje|qq=}}, with potential descendants such as {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]]. However, he was not a member of the [[:en:Baekje|Baekje royal family]] of the Korean Peninsula but rather a descendant of Emperor Bidatsu.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea}} [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kogane Kofun (Kawaran-machi)
Nearby stands the monument of a poem by Te-mochi Princess.]] In the first year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ''[[朱鳥 (era)|Shuchō era]]'' ([[:en:686|686]]), Prince Kawachi was dispatched to [[Tsukushi Province]] with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakatomi no Ōshima|ja|藤原大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to host Kim Chisang, an envoy from [[Silla]]. At the time, he held the Rank of ''Jōkōshi''. In [[September]] of the same year, upon the emperor’s passing, Prince Kawachi delivered eulogies for the [[Ōtoneri Bureau|great chamberlain]] and was likely in charge of related offices. In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:689|689]]), he was appointed [[Dazai no Sochi]] in Tsukushi and was granted weapons and goods. In [[:en:690|690]], he oversaw the hosting of [[Silla]] envoys. In [[:en:692|692]], he dispatched [[:en:Buddhist monks|Buddhist monks]] to spread [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] to [[:en:Ōsumi Province|Ōsumi Province]] and [[Ata Province]], as well as ordered the presentation of an [[Amitābha]] statue. Prince Kawachi passed away in [[March]] [[:en:694|694]] while serving in Tsukushi. He was posthumously awarded the Rank of ''Jōdai-shi'' on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], along with funeral offerings. His funeral elegies by {{ill|Te-mochi Princess|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Princess|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, presumed to be his wife, appear in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' Volume 3, poems 417-419. His supposed tomb, the '''Kogane Kofun''' in [[Kawaran-machi]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], was designated as his burial site by the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Ministry of the Imperial Household]] in [[:en:1894|1894]]. However, archaeological studies identify it as a late 6th-century round tumulus. == Prince Kawachi (Nara period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]), a Nara period prince, was the grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].Sawada Hiroshi, "Genealogy of Tenmu's Imperial Relatives" (''Kokushi Gaku'', No. 142, [[:en:1990|1990]]) In the 7th year of the [[Wadō]] era ([[:en:714|714]]), he was promoted from ''unranked'' to ''Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade'' by hereditary privilege. However, he left no notable records and passed away on [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]. While the ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' lists him as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]], these claims conflict with primary sources. == Confusion in Genealogical Records == Although these two figures, Prince Kawachi of the Asuka and Nara periods, lived in different eras, their genealogies were historically conflated. The ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' mistakenly lists {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers as descendants of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].For example, see Yoshikawa Kōbunkan’s ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary'', or annotations in the ''Shin Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei'' edition of the ''Shoku Nihongi''. Modern scholarship, particularly by {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Takashi|qq=}}, corrects this confusion by separating the two figures. He identifies the Asuka Prince Kawachi as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers, while listing the Nara Prince Kawachi as the son of Prince Nagaya.Tanaka Takashi, "On the Genealogy of Prince Takayasu," in ''Tanaka Takashi Collected Works,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * [[Takeuchi Risan]], [[Yamada Hideo]], [[Hirano Kunio]], eds. ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary,'' Volume 2. [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], [[:en:1959|1959]]. * [[Tanaka Takashi]], "Debates on Imperial Genealogies," in ''Collected Works of Tanaka Takashi,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kawachi no Ōkimi]] [[ja:河内王]] [[sn:awachi no Ōkimi]] [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Nara period]] [[Category:People of Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:People of Buzen Province]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Asuka period]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (河内王), also referred to as '''Kawachi-Ō''' (川内王), was a title borne by multiple {{ill|Imperial family|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial family|qq=}} members in ancient Japan. The name appears across different periods, from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], serving as the {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|qq=}}'''. * A princess of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and a [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Joō|qq=}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/694|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|qq=}}, a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kudara|qq=}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中臣大島}} to receive {{ill|Silla envoy|lt=Silla envoy|lt=Envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/envoys from Silla|qq=}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the Rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Joō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, found in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/728|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), active during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], was the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. In {{ill|714|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/714|qq=}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unranked|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|qq=}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda’s descendants|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大原桜井}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kadobe|qq=}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * [[:en:Rizō Takeuchi|Taketoshi Tsurugi]], {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Hideo|qq=}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirano Kunio|qq=}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|qq=}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] ==Merged second translation== '''Prince Kawachi''' (河内王, ''Kawachi no Ōkimi'' or ''Kawachi no Ō'') refers to multiple Japanese [[imperial family|imperial princes]] (諸王, ''shokō'') who lived from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[Heian period]]. He is also referred to as '''Prince Kawauchi''' (川内王). * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], who served as the [[Dazai no Sochi|Governor of Tsukushi]] in [[Kyushu]] during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)|Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An early [[Nara period]] imperial prince, presumed to be the first son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]], the son of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Nara period)]]'''. * A late Nara period imperial prince of unknown lineage. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)]]'''. * A Nara period princess, the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takahata]] and a poet of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. See '''[[Kawachi no Joō]]'''. * Other figures bearing the title of ''Prince Kawachi'' are known, but none are of particular note. == Prince Kawachi (Asuka period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - before [[:en:April 5|April 5]] in the 8th year of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:694|694]])) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although no direct genealogical records exist, analyses from works such as the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' and ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' suggest he was a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] through {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|qq=}}. He may have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Baekje|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Baekje|qq=}}, with potential descendants such as {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]]. However, he was not a member of the [[:en:Baekje|Baekje royal family]] of the Korean Peninsula but rather a descendant of Emperor Bidatsu.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea}} [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kogane Kofun (Kawaran-machi)
Nearby stands the monument of a poem by Te-mochi Princess.]] In the first year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ''[[朱鳥 (era)|Shuchō era]]'' ([[:en:686|686]]), Prince Kawachi was dispatched to [[Tsukushi Province]] with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakatomi no Ōshima|ja|藤原大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to host Kim Chisang, an envoy from [[Silla]]. At the time, he held the Rank of ''Jōkōshi''. In [[September]] of the same year, upon the emperor’s passing, Prince Kawachi delivered eulogies for the [[Ōtoneri Bureau|great chamberlain]] and was likely in charge of related offices. In the third year of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:689|689]]), he was appointed [[Dazai no Sochi]] in Tsukushi and was granted weapons and goods. In [[:en:690|690]], he oversaw the hosting of [[Silla]] envoys. In [[:en:692|692]], he dispatched [[:en:Buddhist monks|Buddhist monks]] to spread [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] to [[:en:Ōsumi Province|Ōsumi Province]] and [[Ata Province]], as well as ordered the presentation of an [[Amitābha]] statue. Prince Kawachi passed away in [[March]] [[:en:694|694]] while serving in Tsukushi. He was posthumously awarded the Rank of ''Jōdai-shi'' on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], along with funeral offerings. His funeral elegies by {{ill|Te-mochi Princess|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Princess|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, presumed to be his wife, appear in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' Volume 3, poems 417-419. His supposed tomb, the '''Kogane Kofun''' in [[Kawaran-machi]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], was designated as his burial site by the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Ministry of the Imperial Household]] in [[:en:1894|1894]]. However, archaeological studies identify it as a late 6th-century round tumulus. == Prince Kawachi (Nara period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]), a Nara period prince, was the grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].Sawada Hiroshi, "Genealogy of Tenmu's Imperial Relatives" (''Kokushi Gaku'', No. 142, [[:en:1990|1990]]) In the 7th year of the [[Wadō]] era ([[:en:714|714]]), he was promoted from ''unranked'' to ''Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade'' by hereditary privilege. However, he left no notable records and passed away on [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]. While the ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' lists him as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]], these claims conflict with primary sources. == Confusion in Genealogical Records == Although these two figures, Prince Kawachi of the Asuka and Nara periods, lived in different eras, their genealogies were historically conflated. The ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' mistakenly lists {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers as descendants of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].For example, see Yoshikawa Kōbunkan’s ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary'', or annotations in the ''Shin Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei'' edition of the ''Shoku Nihongi''. Modern scholarship, particularly by {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Takashi|qq=}}, corrects this confusion by separating the two figures. He identifies the Asuka Prince Kawachi as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers, while listing the Nara Prince Kawachi as the son of Prince Nagaya.Tanaka Takashi, "On the Genealogy of Prince Takayasu," in ''Tanaka Takashi Collected Works,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * [[Takeuchi Risan]], [[Yamada Hideo]], [[Hirano Kunio]], eds. ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary,'' Volume 2. [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], [[:en:1959|1959]]. * [[Tanaka Takashi]], "Debates on Imperial Genealogies," in ''Collected Works of Tanaka Takashi,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kawachi no Ōkimi]] [[ja:河内王]] [[sn:awachi no Ōkimi]] [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Nara period]] [[Category:People of Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:People of Buzen Province]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Asuka period]]

The name appears across different periods, from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and refers to various individuals: * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], serving as the {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. See '''[[#Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period)]]'''. * An imperial prince of the late [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], whose lineage is unclear. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E7%8E%8B_(%E5%A5%88%E8%89%AF%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3%E5%BE%8C%E6%9C%9F)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Ōkimi (Late Nara period)|qq=}}'''. * A princess of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and a [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] poet. See '''{{ill|Kawachi no Joō|ja|%E6%B2%B3%E5%86%85%E5%A5%B3%E7%8E%8B|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi no Joō|qq=}}'''. * Other individuals bearing the title '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' are known, but none are particularly notable. == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Asuka period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – April {{ill|694|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/694|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although his exact genealogy is not documented in reliable historical sources, analysis of texts like the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] suggests he was part of the lineage of {{ill|Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe|lt=Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Oe|qq=}}, a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]]. Some interpretations suggest he was the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Kudara|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kudara|qq=}}, who, despite his name, was not connected to the Korean kingdom of Baekje{{Cite news|author=|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea|newspaper=|publisher=}}. [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawachi no Ōkimi’s tomb site (Fukuoka Prefecture)
Includes the epitaph of Te-mochi Joō]] In 686, he was dispatched to Tsukushi with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Fujiwara no Ōshima|ja|中臣大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中臣大島}} to receive {{ill|Silla envoy|lt=Silla envoy|lt=Envoys from Silla|ja|新羅使|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/envoys from Silla|qq=}}. In this capacity, he served as an intermediary for Emperor Tenmu. By 689, he was appointed {{ill|Dazai no Sochi|ja|筑紫大宰帥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Tsukushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰帥}} under [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. In 694, he passed away in Tsukushi and was posthumously promoted to the Rank of {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|浄大肆|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. His funeral rites were held at Mount Kagami in Bungo Province, as documented in three elegiac poems by his wife, {{ill|Te-mochi Joō|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Joō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, found in the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]], Volume 3 (poems 417–419). == Kawachi no Ōkimi (Nara period) == '''Kawachi no Ōkimi''' (birthdate unknown – July {{ill|728|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/728|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), active during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], was the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]]. In {{ill|714|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/714|qq=}}, he was promoted from {{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unranked|qq=}} to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}} as a grandson of Emperor Tenmu. He passed away in 728 without leaving significant achievements. The genealogical relationship between the two individuals known as Kawachi no Ōkimi has historically been confused, with figures such as [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takeda]] and his descendants inaccurately merged into their lineage. == Genealogical Confusion == Genealogical confusion arose from sources such as the {{ill|Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|ja|本朝皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō Kōin Shōunroku|qq=}}, which merged the two individuals into a single lineage. The record erroneously placed {{ill|Prince Takeda’s descendants|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda’s descendants|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} into the lineage of Kawachi no Ōkimi of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], including figures like {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|桜井王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大原桜井}}, and {{ill|Prince Kadobe|ja|門部王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kadobe|qq=}}. Modern scholars, including Tanaka Taku, have reconstructed the genealogies to distinguish between the two individuals, separating the lines into those descended from [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] and those descended from {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}Tanaka Taku, "On the Debate Concerning Prince Kibi," *Collected Works of Tanaka Taku,* Vol. 5, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985.. == References == {{Reflist}} == Sources == * [[:en:Rizō Takeuchi|Taketoshi Tsurugi]], {{ill|Yamada Hideo|ja|山田英雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Hideo|qq=}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirano Kunio|qq=}}, eds., *Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary*, Vol. 2, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1959, ISBN 9784642020022. * Ichimura Hiroshi, "Kawachi no Ōkimi in Man'yōshū (Special Lecture 198)," *Dimensions,* Vol. 24, No. 10, October 1978. * {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|qq=}}, *Studies on Prince Nagaya*, Kokusho Kankōkai, 1985, ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] }} [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:河内王]] ==Merged second translation== '''Prince Kawachi''' (河内王, ''Kawachi no Ōkimi'' or ''Kawachi no Ō'') refers to multiple Japanese [[imperial family|imperial princes]] (諸王, ''shokō'') who lived from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. He is also referred to as '''Prince Kawauchi''' (川内王). * An imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], who served as the [[Dazai no Sochi|Governor of Tsukushi]] in [[Kyushu]] during the reign of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)|Prince Kawachi (Asuka period)]]'''. * An early [[Nara period]] imperial prince, presumed to be the first son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]], the son of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Nara period)]]'''. * A late Nara period imperial prince of unknown lineage. See '''[[#Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)|Prince Kawachi (Late Nara period)]]'''. * A Nara period princess, the daughter of [[:en:Prince Takechi|Prince Takahata]] and a poet of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]''. See '''[[Kawachi no Joō]]'''. * Other figures bearing the title of ''Prince Kawachi'' are known, but none are of particular note. == Prince Kawachi (Asuka period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - before [[:en:April 5|April 5]] in the 8th year of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:694|694]])) was an imperial prince of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]]. Although no direct genealogical records exist, analyses from works such as the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' and ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' suggest he was a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] through {{ill|Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|ja|押坂彦人大兄皇子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Oshisaka no Hikohito no Ōe|qq=}}. He may have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Prince Baekje|ja|百済王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Baekje|qq=}}, with potential descendants such as {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]]. However, he was not a member of the [[:en:Baekje|Baekje royal family]] of the Korean Peninsula but rather a descendant of Emperor Bidatsu.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141022115646/http://www.geocities.jp/namu_mauru/daiou6.htm|title=Ancient Japan and Korea}} [[File:Tomb of Prince Kawachi.jpg|thumb|200px|Kogane Kofun (Kawaran-machi)
Nearby stands the monument of a poem by Te-mochi Princess.]] In the first year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ''[[朱鳥 (era)|Shuchō era]]'' ([[:en:686|686]]), Prince Kawachi was dispatched to [[Tsukushi Province]] with {{ill|Otomo no Otomaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasumaro|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakatomi no Ōshima|ja|藤原大嶋|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ōshima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to host Kim Chisang, an envoy from [[Silla]]. At the time, he held the Rank of ''Jōkōshi''. In [[September]] of the same year, upon the emperor’s passing, Prince Kawachi delivered eulogies for the [[Ōtoneri Bureau|great chamberlain]] and was likely in charge of related offices. In the third year of [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]]’s reign ([[:en:689|689]]), he was appointed [[Dazai no Sochi]] in Tsukushi and was granted weapons and goods. In [[:en:690|690]], he oversaw the hosting of [[Silla]] envoys. In [[:en:692|692]], he dispatched [[:en:Buddhist monks|Buddhist monks]] to spread [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] to [[:en:Ōsumi Province|Ōsumi Province]] and [[Ata Province]], as well as ordered the presentation of an [[Amitābha]] statue. Prince Kawachi passed away in [[March]] [[:en:694|694]] while serving in Tsukushi. He was posthumously awarded the Rank of ''Jōdai-shi'' on [[:en:April 5|April 5]], along with funeral offerings. His funeral elegies by {{ill|Te-mochi Princess|ja|手持女王|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Te-mochi Princess|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, presumed to be his wife, appear in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' Volume 3, poems 417-419. His supposed tomb, the '''Kogane Kofun''' in [[Kawaran-machi]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], was designated as his burial site by the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Ministry of the Imperial Household]] in [[:en:1894|1894]]. However, archaeological studies identify it as a late 6th-century round tumulus. == Prince Kawachi (Nara period) == Prince Kawachi (birth date unknown - [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]), a Nara period prince, was the grandson of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and presumed to be the eldest son of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].Sawada Hiroshi, "Genealogy of Tenmu's Imperial Relatives" (''Kokushi Gaku'', No. 142, [[:en:1990|1990]]) In the 7th year of the [[Wadō]] era ([[:en:714|714]]), he was promoted from ''unranked'' to ''Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade'' by hereditary privilege. However, he left no notable records and passed away on [[July 19]], [[:en:728|728]]. While the ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' lists him as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}}, {{ill|Prince Sakurai|ja|大原桜井|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sakurai|qq=}}, and [[:en:Prince Kadobe|Prince Kadobe]], these claims conflict with primary sources. == Confusion in Genealogical Records == Although these two figures, Prince Kawachi of the Asuka and Nara periods, lived in different eras, their genealogies were historically conflated. The ''{{ill|Kōin Shōunroku|ja|皇胤紹運録|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōin Shōunroku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本朝皇胤紹運録}}'' mistakenly lists {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers as descendants of [[:en:Prince Naga|Prince Nagaya]].For example, see Yoshikawa Kōbunkan’s ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary'', or annotations in the ''Shin Nihon Koten Bungaku Taikei'' edition of the ''Shoku Nihongi''. Modern scholarship, particularly by {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Takashi|qq=}}, corrects this confusion by separating the two figures. He identifies the Asuka Prince Kawachi as the father of {{ill|Prince Takayasu|ja|大原高安|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takayasu|qq=}} and his brothers, while listing the Nara Prince Kawachi as the son of Prince Nagaya.Tanaka Takashi, "On the Genealogy of Prince Takayasu," in ''Tanaka Takashi Collected Works,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * [[Takeuchi Risan]], [[Yamada Hideo]], [[Hirano Kunio]], eds. ''Japanese Ancient Biographical Dictionary,'' Volume 2. [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], [[:en:1959|1959]]. * [[Tanaka Takashi]], "Debates on Imperial Genealogies," in ''Collected Works of Tanaka Takashi,'' Volume 5, Kokushokankōkai, [[:en:1985|1985]]. ISBN 9784336016140. {{translated page|ja|河内王|version=90196833|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:694 deaths]] [[Category:728 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Kawachi no Ōkimi]] [[ja:河内王]] [[sn:awachi no Ōkimi]] [[sn:Kawachi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Nara period]] [[Category:People of Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:People of Buzen Province]] [[Category:Princes of Japan in the Asuka period]]
